name: Field of Reeds
description: The Field of Reeds, also known as Aaru, is the paradise of the afterlife in ancient Egyptian mythology. It is a mirror image of the mortal world, but more fertile and abundant. It is believed that after passing through the trials of the Duat and being judged by Osiris, worthy souls are granted eternal life in the Field of Reeds. Here, they continue their earthly activities, farming, feasting, and enjoying the company of loved ones, but without the hardships and suffering of mortal existence. The landscape is described as lush and verdant, filled with rivers, lakes, and of course, fields of reeds. It is a place of eternal peace and happiness, a reward for a life well-lived. The Field of Reeds represents the Egyptians' belief in the continuity of life after death and the importance of living a moral life to attain this blissful afterlife. It is a place where the soul can truly flourish and find eternal contentment. The journey to Aaru is fraught with peril, but the ultimate reward is well worth the struggle. The deceased are often depicted working in the fields, emphasizing the active nature of the afterlife. This idyllic vision of paradise played a crucial role in shaping Egyptian morality and religious practices.
subtitle: Egyptian Paradise and Afterlife Realm

keyMyths.title: Journey to Aaru
keyMyths.description: The soul must navigate the Duat and be judged by Osiris to gain access to the Field of Reeds. Only those with a pure heart are granted entry.

keyMyths.title: Life in Aaru
keyMyths.description: Life in the Field of Reeds is a continuation of earthly existence, but without suffering. The deceased farm the land and enjoy eternal happiness.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: Translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.
sources.text: Translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.

sources.author: James P. Allen

sources.description: Provides context to the Egyptian afterlife beliefs.
